stem: You are the obstetric registrar attending a vaginal birth of a 32-year-old G3P2 at 40+3 weeks. Her two previous babies weighed 3900 g and 4100 g. The current pregnancy was uncomplicated and her clinically estimated fetal weight at 39 weeks was 4000 g. After delivery of the head there is a turtle-neck sign, restitution does not occur and routine axial traction fails to deliver the anterior shoulder. The clock starts. The midwife immediately calls for senior assistance and a neonatal team.

data_table.Vital signs: Mother BP 122/76, HR 108; Fetal HR 130 prior
data_table.Examination: Head delivered, turtle-neck sign, no restitution, anterior shoulder behind pubic symphysis, perineum intact

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step after calling for help?

options.text: Perform a generous mediolateral episiotomy as the immediate first action because more vaginal space is the primary mechanical solution to shoulder dystocia regardless of the shoulder-pelvis relationship and the perineum is the rate-limiting structure
options.rationale: Episiotomy alone does not relieve bony obstruction at the pubic symphysis; it may help access for internal manoeuvres but is not the first step. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder dystocia)

options.text: Hyperflex maternal hips onto the abdomen in McRoberts position and apply firm suprapubic pressure directed posteriorly and laterally with axial traction on the fetal head to attempt anterior shoulder release
options.rationale: RANZCOG and RCOG HELPERR algorithm starts with McRoberts and suprapubic pressure as first-line manoeuvres because they resolve over 50 percent of shoulder dystocias with low complication rates. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder dystocia)

options.text: Apply firm fundal pressure with strong downward axial traction on the head and ask the mother to push vigorously for 30 seconds to dislodge the anterior shoulder by combined uterine and maternal expulsive forces
options.rationale: Fundal pressure worsens shoulder dystocia by impacting the anterior shoulder further against the symphysis and is contraindicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder dystocia)

options.text: Move directly to the Zavanelli manoeuvre by cephalic replacement and emergency caesarean section as the safest first action whenever the anterior shoulder is impacted because internal manoeuvres carry unacceptably high brachial plexus injury risk
options.rationale: Zavanelli is reserved for failure of all first and second-line manoeuvres; it has high morbidity and is not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder dystocia)

options.text: Cut the umbilical cord and proceed with prompt vaginal extraction with traction below the horizontal because cord transection ends the time-critical hypoxia and provides immediate tactile access to the shoulder
options.rationale: Cord cutting at this stage offers no benefit while the head is delivered; the priority is mechanical disimpaction of the shoulder. (Source: RANZCOG: Shoulder dystocia)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia is the inability to deliver the anterior shoulder with routine axial traction after delivery of the head. It is an obstetric emergency requiring immediate, systematic management following the HELPERR mnemonic. Step 1: call for HELP. Step 2: consider Episiotomy (to assist internal manoeuvres if needed). Step 3: Legs in McRoberts (hyperflexion at hips onto maternal abdomen). Step 4: Pressure suprapubically (Rubin I, directed posteriorly and laterally). Step 5: Enter pelvis for internal rotational manoeuvres (Rubin II, Woods corkscrew, reverse Woods). Step 6: Remove posterior arm. Step 7: Roll patient to all-fours (Gaskin manoeuvre). Documentation of time elapsed at each step is mandatory.
key_points: Call for help and start the clock
McRoberts + suprapubic pressure: first-line, resolves over 50 percent
Avoid fundal pressure (impacts the shoulder)
Internal manoeuvres: Rubin II, Woods, reverse Woods, posterior arm delivery
Last resort: Zavanelli or symphysiotomy
explanation.pearls: Brachial plexus injury is associated with both excessive lateral traction on the head and prolonged shoulder dystocia; calm, controlled progression through HELPERR minimises injury risk.

subtopic_detail: Gestational trophoblastic neoplasia after complete mole - referral and chemotherapy
stem: You are the gynaecology registrar following a 32-year-old woman who underwent suction evacuation of a complete hydatidiform mole 9 weeks ago. Histology confirmed a complete mole with diploid 46,XX karyotype. Her serum beta-hCG fell from 92 000 IU/L pre-evacuation to 4200 at 4 weeks, 3850 at 5 weeks, 3900 at 6 weeks and now 4050 IU/L at 7 weeks post-evacuation. She has had no abnormal vaginal bleeding and feels well. A transvaginal ultrasound today shows a 2 cm intramyometrial mass with increased vascularity. Chest X-ray is clear.

data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, no organomegaly, normal speculum, no abnormal bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Serial b-hCG plateau over 3 weeks at 3850 to 4200 IU/L, TVS: 2 cm intramyometrial mass with high vascular flow, CXR clear, FBC/UEC/LFTs normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer urgently to a tertiary gestational trophoblastic disease service for FIGO 2000 staging, complete metastatic workup including CT chest abdomen pelvis and MRI brain, and consideration of single-agent methotrexate chemotherapy for low-risk gestational trophoblastic neoplasia
options.rationale: FIGO criteria for GTN after molar pregnancy include a plateau of hCG over 4 measurements across at least 3 weeks (days 1, 7, 14, 21), so this woman has GTN; tertiary referral, staging and stratified chemotherapy (single-agent MTX for low-risk, EMA-CO for high-risk) is the standard pathway. (Source: FIGO 2018: Gestational Trophoblastic Neoplasia)

options.text: Repeat the serum beta-hCG in 4 weeks and only consider further investigation or referral if the value rises by more than 50 percent or if she develops symptoms of recurrent disease such as bleeding, dyspnoea or central neurological deficits
options.rationale: FIGO criteria for diagnosis of post-molar GTN are already met (plateau of 4 measurements over 3 weeks); waiting another 4 weeks delays curative chemotherapy. (Source: FIGO 2018: Gestational Trophoblastic Neoplasia)

options.text: Arrange repeat suction curettage of the uterine cavity as definitive treatment for the intramyometrial mass without referral to a tertiary centre or chemotherapy because single-agent chemotherapy carries unacceptable bone marrow toxicity at her age
options.rationale: Repeat curettage is reserved for selected cases of persistent intracavitary trophoblastic tissue; the plateau in hCG with an intramyometrial mass requires chemotherapy rather than repeat surgery alone. (Source: FIGO 2018: Gestational Trophoblastic Neoplasia)

options.text: Perform a total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy as definitive treatment of post-molar gestational trophoblastic disease because chemotherapy carries unacceptable risks of long-term secondary malignancy in young women
options.rationale: Fertility-sparing chemotherapy cures over 95 percent of low-risk post-molar GTN; hysterectomy is reserved for women who have completed family or for chemoresistant disease. (Source: FIGO 2018: Gestational Trophoblastic Neoplasia)

options.text: Commence the combination chemotherapy regimen EMA-CO (etoposide methotrexate actinomycin-D cyclophosphamide vincristine) immediately at the local oncology service as initial empirical therapy without further staging or referral to a national GTD centre
options.rationale: EMA-CO is reserved for high-risk GTN (FIGO score 7 or above), CNS or hepatic metastases or methotrexate failure; low-risk patients receive single-agent MTX or actinomycin-D after staging. (Source: FIGO 2018: Gestational Trophoblastic Neoplasia)

explanation.summary: Gestational trophoblastic neoplasia (GTN) after molar pregnancy is diagnosed by FIGO criteria: (1) plateau in serum hCG over 4 measurements during at least 3 weeks, (2) rise in hCG by 10 percent or more over 3 measurements during at least 2 weeks, (3) histological evidence of choriocarcinoma, or (4) persistence of hCG above 6 months. Staging uses FIGO anatomic stage and modified WHO prognostic score (age, antecedent pregnancy, interval, hCG, tumour size, sites and number of metastases, prior chemo). Score under 7 = low-risk treated with single-agent methotrexate or actinomycin-D. Score 7+ or stage IV = high-risk treated with EMA-CO. Australian women are referred to specialist tertiary GTD centres for staging and treatment, with monitoring by serial hCG to undetectable.
key_points: FIGO post-molar GTN diagnosis: plateau (4 over 3 weeks) or rise (10%+ over 2 weeks)
Stage by CXR, pelvic US, then CT/MRI if indicated
WHO prognostic score: under 7 = low-risk (single agent MTX)
7+ or distant mets = high-risk (EMA-CO)
Strict contraception during and 12 months after chemotherapy
explanation.pearls: Phantom hCG from heterophile antibodies (rarely with hook effect at very high titres) is a key DDx in apparent plateau without imaging findings; urine hCG and serial dilution rule it out before committing to chemotherapy.

subtopic_detail: First-episode schizophrenia antipsychotic selection and monitoring
stem: You are the psychiatry registrar in an early-psychosis service reviewing a 21-year-old engineering student who was admitted involuntarily 2 weeks ago after a first-episode psychosis with 6 months of persecutory delusions and auditory hallucinations. He has fully cooperated with treatment, has no medical comorbidities and his BMI is 24. He has gained 1 kg in two weeks and his symptoms are partially improved on oral olanzapine 10 mg nocte. He smokes 10 cigarettes per day and has no family history of diabetes or cardiac disease.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72, HR 84, BMI 24, weight 76 kg (up from 75)
data_table.Examination: Affect blunted but engaged, no EPS, no abnormal involuntary movements, normal cardiovascular and neurological examination
data_table.Investigations: Baseline ECG QTc 412 ms, FBC, UEC, LFTs normal, fasting lipids: total cholesterol 4.6, LDL 2.8, fasting glucose 5.0 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.4%, prolactin 410 mIU/L (slightly elevated)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue oral olanzapine at the current dose, provide brief smoking cessation advice with nicotine replacement therapy and arrange baseline-plus-3-monthly metabolic monitoring with weight, waist circumference, fasting lipids and glucose for at least the first year of treatment
options.rationale: RANZCP and Therapeutic Guidelines Psychotropic recommend continuing the initial effective antipsychotic with structured metabolic monitoring (weight monthly for 3 months then 3-monthly, lipids and glucose at baseline, 3 months and 12 months) for all first-episode patients on second-generation agents. (Source: RANZCP: Clinical practice guidelines for the management of schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP: Clinical practice guidelines for the management of schizophrenia

options.text: Switch immediately to first-generation haloperidol 5 mg daily because second-generation antipsychotics in young adult males consistently produce greater metabolic and prolactin morbidity than first-generation agents at equivalent symptomatic doses
options.rationale: FGAs cause more EPS and tardive dyskinesia and similar long-term metabolic concerns; switching after a partial response in 2 weeks is premature. (Source: RANZCP: Clinical practice guidelines for the management of schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP: Clinical practice guidelines for the management of schizophrenia

options.text: Add a mood stabiliser such as sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily as augmentation for partial response at 2 weeks because combination therapy from the outset shortens time to remission in first-episode schizophrenia and reduces relapse risk
options.rationale: Valproate is not first-line augmentation for first-episode schizophrenia and adds teratogenicity, weight gain and hepatotoxicity risks without clear benefit at 2 weeks. (Source: RANZCP: Clinical practice guidelines for the management of schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP: Clinical practice guidelines for the management of schizophrenia

options.text: Discontinue antipsychotic medication entirely and commence weekly cognitive behavioural therapy alone because psychological therapy is now first-line in first-episode psychosis if symptoms partially improve during the initial 2-week admission period
options.rationale: CBT for psychosis is an adjunct to antipsychotic medication, not a substitute; discontinuation risks rapid relapse in first-episode schizophrenia. (Source: RANZCP: Clinical practice guidelines for the management of schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP: Clinical practice guidelines for the management of schizophrenia

options.text: Start a long-acting injectable depot antipsychotic such as paliperidone palmitate 150 mg deep IM at first dose, omit further oral antipsychotic and discharge home without further specialist follow up because depot reduces relapse risk most effectively
options.rationale: LAI is appropriate in selected first-episode patients with adherence concerns, but is given alongside specialist multidisciplinary follow up, not discharge without it. (Source: RANZCP: Clinical practice guidelines for the management of schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP: Clinical practice guidelines for the management of schizophrenia

explanation.summary: First-episode schizophrenia is treated with a second-generation antipsychotic at the lowest effective dose, allowed at least 4 to 6 weeks to assess full response. Olanzapine, risperidone, aripiprazole and amisulpride are common first-line choices; clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant illness after two failed trials. Baseline workup includes ECG (QTc), FBC, UEC, LFTs, fasting lipids, glucose, prolactin and weight/waist. RANZCP monitoring is weekly weight for 6 weeks, lipids and glucose at baseline, 3 months and 12 months, prolactin if symptomatic. Smoking cessation, exercise, dietitian and metabolic syndrome prevention are integral. CBT for psychosis, family therapy and supported employment are added.
key_points: SGA at lowest effective dose; allow 4-6 weeks for full effect
Baseline: ECG (QTc), FBC, UEC, LFTs, lipids, glucose, prolactin, weight
Monitor weight weekly to 6 weeks then 3-monthly
Lipids and glucose at baseline, 3 months, 12 months
Clozapine after two failed trials
explanation.pearls: Olanzapine has the largest weight gain and metabolic effect of common SGAs; aripiprazole is most weight-neutral but more activating. Prolactin elevation is greatest with risperidone and amisulpride.

sources.label: RANZCP: Clinical practice guidelines for the management of schizophrenia

subtopic_detail: Serotonin syndrome from SSRI plus tramadol
stem: You are the consult-liaison psychiatry registrar called by the emergency department about a 48-year-old man on sertraline 200 mg daily for major depressive disorder who attended his general practitioner two days ago for low back pain and was prescribed tramadol 50 mg three times daily. Today he presents with tremor, agitation, sweating, dilated pupils and inducible ankle clonus that is more marked on the right. His vital signs show hyperthermia at 38.6 degrees C, tachycardia and hypertension. He has had no recent alcohol or illicit drug use.

data_table.Examination: Tremor, hyperreflexia greater in lower limbs, inducible ankle clonus, dilated pupils, diaphoretic, agitated and restless, mild abdominal hyperactive bowel sounds
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, CK 320 U/L, UEC normal, ECG sinus tachycardia QTc 420 ms, toxicology screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Commence intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g infusion and oral propranolol 40 mg three times daily as first-line therapy for serotonin syndrome and continue both serotonergic agents at reduced doses with ongoing twice-daily monitoring of vital signs
options.rationale: Magnesium and beta-blockers are not first-line; cessation of serotonergic agents and benzodiazepines are the foundation of treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology - Serotonin syndrome)

options.text: Continue both medications because tramadol plus an SSRI does not produce clinically significant serotonin syndrome at standard doses and treat the autonomic features with intravenous beta blocker and oral paracetamol for the hyperthermia
options.rationale: Tramadol is a known serotonergic agent and combination with high-dose SSRI causes serotonin syndrome; treating symptoms without ceasing the precipitants risks progression. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology - Serotonin syndrome)

options.text: Treat as neuroleptic malignant syndrome with intravenous dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg and oral bromocriptine 2.5 mg every 8 hours and arrange transfer to intensive care because the autonomic instability is identical to NMS in clinical presentation
options.rationale: NMS is associated with antipsychotic dopamine antagonism, with lead-pipe rigidity not the lower-limb-predominant clonus, hyperreflexia and dilated pupils of serotonin syndrome. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology - Serotonin syndrome)

options.text: Cease sertraline and tramadol immediately, provide intravenous benzodiazepine sedation with diazepam or midazolam, active cooling and supportive intravenous fluids, and consider oral cyproheptadine 12 mg as a serotonin antagonist if symptoms persist
options.rationale: Serotonin syndrome management is cessation of all serotonergic agents, benzodiazepine sedation for agitation, active cooling, IV hydration; cyproheptadine 12 mg orally as a 5-HT2A antagonist is added for moderate to severe cases per Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology - Serotonin syndrome)

options.text: Administer single dose intravenous physostigmine 1 mg over 5 minutes as the specific cholinergic antidote for serotonin syndrome and continue intravenous hydration only with no further pharmacological cessation or substitution of any kind
options.rationale: Physostigmine is the antidote for anticholinergic toxicity, not serotonin syndrome; the dilated pupils, sweating and hyperactive bowels here are pro-serotonergic. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology - Serotonin syndrome)

explanation.summary: Serotonin syndrome is a clinical diagnosis using the Hunter Criteria: serotonergic agent plus one of spontaneous clonus, inducible clonus with agitation or diaphoresis, ocular clonus, tremor and hyperreflexia, or hypertonia with temperature above 38 and either ocular or inducible clonus. Lower-limb-predominant clonus and hyperreflexia distinguish it from neuroleptic malignant syndrome (lead-pipe rigidity). Management is cessation of all serotonergic agents, benzodiazepine sedation, active cooling, IV hydration, and cyproheptadine 12 mg PO load then 2 mg every 2 hours for moderate to severe cases (max 32 mg/day). ICU and paralysis with intubation are required for hyperthermia over 41.1 degrees C.
key_points: Hunter Criteria: serotonergic agent + characteristic neuromuscular signs
Clue: clonus and hyperreflexia greater in lower limbs
Cease all serotonergic agents (SSRIs, SNRIs, tramadol, linezolid, MAOIs)
Benzodiazepines first-line for agitation and autonomic features
Cyproheptadine 12 mg PO load, 2 mg q2h for moderate to severe
explanation.pearls: Common SSRI plus tramadol is a high-yield combination at GP-prescribing interfaces; education at the SSRI prescription stage helps the patient flag risk if subsequently offered tramadol.

subtopic_detail: Inpatient alcohol withdrawal management with CIWA-Ar guidance
stem: You are the medical registrar admitting a 52-year-old man with daily heavy alcohol use (estimated 14 standard drinks per day for 12 years) who has come in for elective inpatient detoxification. His last drink was 14 hours ago. He has no history of seizures or delirium tremens. He is fully oriented but tremulous and anxious. His CIWA-Ar score is 18 currently. He has no other medical comorbidities, no known liver disease and no pregnancy is possible.

data_table.Examination: Coarse tremor of outstretched hands, mildly anxious, mild diaphoresis, oriented to time place and person, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, LFTs ALT 78 AST 96 GGT 240, INR 1.0, UEC normal, BSL 5.4, ethanol level 0.04% on admission, CIWA-Ar 18

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Commence symptom-triggered oral diazepam 10 to 20 mg every 1 to 2 hours guided by CIWA-Ar score, plus intramuscular thiamine 300 mg daily for 3 days before oral thiamine 100 mg three times daily, and monitor for delirium tremens or seizure
options.rationale: RACP and Therapeutic Guidelines Addiction recommend symptom-triggered diazepam (or chlordiazepoxide) loading guided by CIWA-Ar at or above 8 to 10, with parenteral thiamine 300 mg IM/IV daily for at least 3 days before oral, and monitoring for AWS complications. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction - Alcohol withdrawal)

options.text: Commence oral chlorpromazine 100 mg every 6 hours as first-line therapy because chlorpromazine treats both the autonomic features and any subsequent delirium tremens with a lower risk of respiratory depression than benzodiazepines
options.rationale: Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold and do not prevent the GABA-mediated complications of withdrawal; benzodiazepines are first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction - Alcohol withdrawal)

options.text: Commence intravenous 10% dextrose at maintenance rate prior to thiamine and continue without benzodiazepine cover because adequate carbohydrate restoration alone will prevent Wernicke encephalopathy and reduce the severity of subsequent withdrawal symptoms
options.rationale: Glucose before thiamine in a chronic alcohol user precipitates Wernicke encephalopathy; thiamine before any glucose is the order. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction - Alcohol withdrawal)

options.text: Commence oral baclofen 10 mg three times daily and oral acamprosate 666 mg three times daily for relapse prevention before the patient develops further withdrawal features and as initial monotherapy without benzodiazepine cover during the acute period
options.rationale: Baclofen and acamprosate are relapse-prevention agents started after acute withdrawal is complete, not during it; benzodiazepines are needed for acute management. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction - Alcohol withdrawal)

options.text: Commence a small daily ration of oral alcohol via 30 mL whisky four times daily and gradual taper over 14 days as the safest method of avoiding the GABAergic rebound seen with abrupt cessation of long-term heavy alcohol use
options.rationale: Alcohol tapering is not endorsed in Australian inpatient detox protocols; benzodiazepine cover is the standard substitute. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Addiction - Alcohol withdrawal)

explanation.summary: Acute alcohol withdrawal management uses symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing guided by the CIWA-Ar score (or equivalent AWS scale). Diazepam 10 to 20 mg orally every 1 to 2 hours is given until CIWA-Ar falls below 8 to 10. Parenteral thiamine 300 mg IM or IV daily for at least 3 days then 100 mg orally three times daily prevents Wernicke encephalopathy. Glucose-containing fluids must be preceded by thiamine. Magnesium and folate are replaced if low. Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold and are avoided unless severe agitation persists despite adequate benzodiazepine. Severe AWS, history of delirium tremens or seizures or significant medical comorbidity warrant ICU consideration.
key_points: CIWA-Ar score 8-10 triggers benzodiazepine dosing
Diazepam 10-20 mg PO q1-2h symptom-triggered
Thiamine 300 mg parenteral daily for at least 3 days before glucose
Avoid antipsychotics (lower seizure threshold)
ICU if severe, prior DT or seizures, comorbidity
explanation.pearls: Phenobarbital is reserved for benzodiazepine-refractory withdrawal under specialist supervision; propofol intubation is used in delirium tremens not controlled by maximal benzodiazepines.

sources.label: Australian guidelines for the treatment of alcohol problems (NHMRC)
